mappings.rationaleAI: Death is the second titular concept of this section; the article covers the irreversible cessation of biological functions (A: death as irreparable disruption of life processes), including its biology, causes, and the distinction from disease (the process of disruption leading to death).

Disease is the primary subject of this section's outline; the article covers noncommunicable disease including metabolic and hereditary types (A.1), communicable disease with etiology and pathogenesis (A.2), endemic and epidemic patterns (A.2.b), and prevention and treatment (A.4).

Asthma is a chronic noncommunicable inflammatory disease with environmental and metabolic components (A.1), exemplifying the category of noninfectious disease caused by immune dysregulation and environmental triggers.

Myocardial infarction is a leading noncommunicable disease caused by metabolic and environmental risk factors including atherosclerosis, hypertension, and smoking (A.1), representing the most common cause of death globally.

Smoking is a major environmental hazard that causes noncommunicable diseases including lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, and COPD (A.1), directly relevant to the metabolic defects and environmental hazards category of disease.
Physiology defines the normal state of organismal function from which disease represents a departure (A), providing the framework for understanding homeostasis disruption that characterizes disease.

Pasteur established the germ theory of communicable disease (A.2), developed vaccines including for anthrax and rabies (A.3, A.4 control), and founded modern bacteriology and immunology, making him central to the scientific understanding of contagious disease and its control.

Infection is the direct subject of communicable disease (A.2), covering the multifactorial invasion by pathogens and host responses (A.2.a), and connecting to endemic and epidemic disease patterns (A.2.b) through epidemiology of infectious diseases.

The common cold is a communicable disease caused by multiple viral pathogens (A.2), exemplifying endemic respiratory infection (A.2.b) with seasonal fluctuation and widespread population-level prevalence.

Influenza exemplifies both endemic seasonal disease and epidemic/pandemic disease (A.2.b) caused by a contagious pathogen (A.2), with documented pandemics and annual epidemic cycles making it a core example of communicable disease.

Ageing is biologically linked to the progressive disruption of homeostasis leading to death (A), with age-related metabolic defects — DNA oxidation, cellular dysfunction — constituting the primary category of noncommunicable disease and accounting for two-thirds of all deaths (A.1).

Health defines the normal state of well-being (A: disease as departure from the normal state or disruption of homeostasis), providing the baseline concept against which disease is defined and measured.

Mental health covers psychological disorders and mental illness as noncommunicable diseases involving metabolic and environmental etiologies (A.1), including neuropsychiatric conditions that rank among the leading causes of global disease burden.

Hygiene covers practices for disease prevention including hand washing, respiratory hygiene, and food safety (A.4: control of disease, prevention), directly addressing the preventive side of communicable disease control.

Nutrition covers deficiency and excess states that produce malnutrition as a noncommunicable disease (A.1), including metabolic consequences of inadequate macro- and micronutrient intake affecting organismal homeostasis.

Radioactive decay produces ionizing radiation as an environmental hazard causing biological damage including cancer and radiation sickness (A.1: environmental hazards as causes of noncommunicable disease).

The immune system is directly referenced in the outline (A.3: immunity), covering innate and adaptive immunity, vaccination, and immune dysfunction (immunodeficiency, autoimmunity) that underlies both protection from and susceptibility to communicable disease (A.2).

Domestication covers the history of animal domestication and the diseases transmitted between domesticated animals and humans (zoonoses), relevant to animal diseases (C) and the transmission of contagious disease across the human-animal interface.

Life defines the biological state — homeostasis, metabolism, reproduction — whose disruption constitutes disease and whose irreparable failure constitutes death (A), providing foundational context for the section's conceptual framework.

Famine causes noncommunicable disease through malnutrition and metabolic deficits (A.1) while simultaneously enabling epidemic disease outbreaks in weakened populations (A.2.b: endemic and epidemic disease), connecting food scarcity to both disease categories.

Violence is a leading cause of death and injury constituting an irreparable disruption of life processes (A), and the article addresses it as a public health concern alongside biological causes of disease and death.

The cell is the fundamental unit whose dysfunction underlies noncommunicable diseases including metabolic defects (A.1); cellular processes such as programmed cell death (apoptosis) and cancer are core to understanding disease at the cellular level.

The brain is the site of neurological and psychiatric noncommunicable diseases (A.1), including stroke, dementia, and brain tumors, and its coverage of neural dysfunction connects to the homeostasis-disruption framework of section A.
